question: You respond to a 45-year-old male at a workplace who coworkers say has been acting strangely for the past 30 minutes. He is confused and combative when you try to examine him. His coworkers mention he is diabetic and takes insulin. He is diaphoretic with pale cool skin and trembling hands. He is unable to answer questions coherently and keeps trying to push you away. Vital signs are BP 138/88 HR 118 RR 22 SpO2 98% on room air. You check his blood glucose and it reads 38 mg/dL. What is occurring and what is the appropriate treatment at the AEMT level?
option_a: He is having a diabetic ketoacidosis episode and needs insulin
option_b: He is hypoglycemic and needs glucose replacement. If he cannot safely swallow give dextrose IV or glucagon IM. Oral glucose is contraindicated due to his altered mental status and aspiration risk
option_c: He is intoxicated and needs supportive care only
option_d: His blood sugar is normal and this is a psychiatric emergency
rationale: Hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose falls below 70 mg/dL with severe symptoms typically below 50 mg/dL. The brain depends entirely on glucose and hypoglycemia causes autonomic symptoms like diaphoresis tremor tachycardia and neuroglycopenic symptoms like confusion bizarre behavior combativeness and eventually seizures and coma. This patient cannot safely swallow due to his altered mental status making oral glucose dangerous due to aspiration risk. At AEMT level establish IV access and give dextrose D10 or D50 per protocol or give glucagon 1 mg IM if IV access is not obtainable. Recheck glucose after treatment.

question: You have established IV access on the hypoglycemic patient. Your protocol allows D10 dextrose 10% for hypoglycemia. How much D10 should you administer to provide the equivalent of 25 grams of dextrose and what is the advantage of D10 over D50?
option_a: Give 25 mL of D10 which equals 25 grams of dextrose
option_b: Give 250 mL of D10 which provides 25 grams of dextrose. D10 is less hyperosmolar than D50 reducing risk of tissue damage if extravasation occurs and allows easier titration
option_c: Give 500 mL of D10 for severe hypoglycemia
option_d: D10 and D50 are identical and interchangeable at the same volume
rationale: D10 contains 10 grams of dextrose per 100 mL so 250 mL provides 25 grams of dextrose. D50 contains 50 grams per 100 mL so 50 mL provides 25 grams. D10 has significant advantages including lower osmolarity which means less tissue damage if the IV infiltrates easier titration to avoid overshooting into hyperglycemia and it can be given through smaller gauge IVs. Many EMS systems have transitioned from D50 to D10 for these reasons. Administer D10 in 100-250 mL increments and recheck blood glucose targeting greater than 70 mg/dL.

question: The hypoglycemic patient has received 250 mL of D10 and his blood glucose is now 94 mg/dL. He is becoming more alert and oriented and is now able to answer questions appropriately. He states he took his morning insulin but then skipped breakfast because he was running late for work. He wants to refuse further care and go back to work. What counseling should you provide?
option_a: He is fine now and can safely refuse care
option_b: Explain that his glucose may drop again because his insulin is still active and he has not eaten. Strongly encourage transport or at minimum he should eat a meal with complex carbohydrates and have someone monitor him. Document his refusal thoroughly if he refuses
option_c: Once blood sugar is corrected there is no risk of recurrence
option_d: He must be transported against his will
rationale: Hypoglycemia can recur especially when caused by insulin or long-acting oral hypoglycemics because the medication continues to act after the glucose is metabolized. This patient took insulin but did not eat so he has active insulin without adequate glucose substrate. If he refuses transport he must understand the risk of recurrent hypoglycemia and should eat a substantial meal containing protein and complex carbohydrates have someone monitor him for the next several hours and seek care immediately if symptoms return. Document your assessment treatment response advice given and his informed refusal.

question: A 62-year-old female with type 2 diabetes is found by her family lethargic and confused. She has been sick with flu-like symptoms for 3 days with vomiting and diarrhea and has not been eating much. Her medications include metformin and glipizide. She is arousable but disoriented. Her skin is dry with poor turgor and her mucous membranes are dry. Vital signs are BP 92/58 HR 112 RR 22 SpO2 96% on room air. Blood glucose is 48 mg/dL. What contributed to her hypoglycemia and what should you do?
option_a: Her metformin caused the hypoglycemia
option_b: Her glipizide a sulfonylurea continued to stimulate insulin release despite reduced food intake causing hypoglycemia. She is also significantly dehydrated. Give IV dextrose and fluid bolus for dehydration
option_c: She has diabetic ketoacidosis from not taking her medications
option_d: Metformin commonly causes hypoglycemia
rationale: Sulfonylureas like glipizide stimulate the pancreas to release insulin regardless of blood glucose levels. When patients are ill and not eating the medication continues working causing hypoglycemia. Metformin does not typically cause hypoglycemia as it works through different mechanisms. This patient is also severely dehydrated from vomiting and diarrhea contributing to her hypotension and tachycardia. Treatment includes IV dextrose for hypoglycemia and IV crystalloid bolus for dehydration. She needs transport for evaluation of both her hypoglycemia and the underlying illness that caused her decreased intake.

question: A 28-year-old male with type 1 diabetes is found by his roommate with altered mental status. The roommate says the patient has been sick for 2 days with abdominal pain nausea and vomiting. The patient is lethargic but arousable and complaining of severe thirst and abdominal pain. His breathing is deep and rapid and you notice a fruity odor on his breath. His skin is warm and dry with poor turgor. Vital signs are BP 96/62 HR 124 RR 28 deep SpO2 97% on room air. Blood glucose reads HIGH on your glucometer which means greater than 500 mg/dL. What diabetic emergency is this and what is the AEMT treatment?
option_a: This is hypoglycemia and he needs dextrose
option_b: This is diabetic ketoacidosis DKA. Treatment includes IV access crystalloid fluid bolus for dehydration and supportive care. Insulin is not given in the field
option_c: This is a simple stomach flu and he just needs antiemetics
option_d: His blood sugar is high so this is not an emergency
rationale: Diabetic ketoacidosis DKA occurs primarily in type 1 diabetics when insulin deficiency prevents glucose from entering cells. The body breaks down fat for energy producing ketones which cause metabolic acidosis. Classic presentation includes hyperglycemia often greater than 300 mg/dL Kussmaul respirations deep rapid breathing to compensate for acidosis fruity breath odor from acetone dehydration from osmotic diuresis abdominal pain and altered mental status. At AEMT level treatment is supportive with IV fluid resuscitation using normal saline 1-2 liter bolus for dehydration. Insulin is a hospital treatment requiring close monitoring. Transport promptly.

question: You are treating the DKA patient and have established IV access and started a normal saline bolus. During transport he becomes increasingly confused and you notice his breathing has become even deeper and more labored. What is the purpose of his Kussmaul respirations and why should you not attempt to slow his breathing?
option_a: The deep breathing is causing his confusion and you should coach him to breathe slower
option_b: Kussmaul respirations are the body's compensation for metabolic acidosis. The deep rapid breathing blows off CO2 to raise blood pH. Slowing respirations would worsen acidosis and could be fatal
option_c: The breathing pattern indicates he is having a panic attack
option_d: He needs a nebulizer treatment for bronchospasm
rationale: Kussmaul respirations are a compensatory mechanism for metabolic acidosis. By breathing deeply and rapidly the patient eliminates carbon dioxide which helps raise blood pH toward normal. The body is essentially using respiratory compensation to offset metabolic acidosis. Any intervention that decreases respiratory rate or depth such as sedation or coaching slow breathing would remove this compensation and cause pH to drop further potentially to fatal levels. The altered mental status is from the acidosis and dehydration not hyperventilation. Continue supportive care and rapid transport for definitive treatment with insulin and continued IV fluids.

question: A 67-year-old male with a history of COPD is sitting on the edge of his bed in obvious respiratory distress. He is using pursed-lip breathing and has his hands on his knees in tripod position. He states his breathing has been getting worse for 3 days despite using his inhalers more frequently. He has a productive cough with thick yellow sputum. Vital signs are BP 152/88 HR 108 RR 28 SpO2 86% on room air. Lung sounds reveal diffuse expiratory wheezing with prolonged expiratory phase and scattered rhonchi. What treatment should you initiate for this COPD exacerbation?
option_a: High-flow oxygen at 15 LPM via non-rebreather mask
option_b: Titrated oxygen to achieve SpO2 88-92% and nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg. Avoid excessive oxygen in COPD patients as it may reduce respiratory drive
option_c: Withhold oxygen because COPD patients should never receive supplemental oxygen
option_d: No treatment is needed since this is his baseline
rationale: COPD exacerbation treatment includes bronchodilators and careful oxygen supplementation. Albuterol 2.5 mg via nebulizer causes bronchodilation relieving airway obstruction. Oxygen should be titrated to SpO2 88-92% in COPD patients because some chronic CO2 retainers depend on hypoxic drive for respiratory stimulation. Excessive oxygen can decrease respiratory drive leading to hypoventilation and CO2 narcosis. However never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient as hypoxia kills faster than hypercarbia. The yellow sputum suggests infection which is a common trigger for COPD exacerbation.

question: You have given the COPD patient a nebulized albuterol treatment and titrated oxygen to achieve SpO2 90%. His wheezing has improved but he remains in moderate distress. His respiratory rate is now 24 and SpO2 is 90% on 4 LPM nasal cannula. He is more comfortable but still working harder than normal to breathe. He is speaking in full sentences now. What additional treatment might benefit this patient if available in your protocols?
option_a: No additional treatment is indicated if he is improving
option_b: If CPAP is available and the patient is alert and cooperative it can significantly reduce work of breathing and improve gas exchange in COPD exacerbation
option_c: Intubation is needed immediately
option_d: Give another albuterol treatment immediately
rationale: CPAP continuous positive airway pressure provides several benefits in COPD exacerbation. The positive pressure splints open airways that tend to collapse during expiration helps recruit collapsed alveoli and reduces the work of breathing. This patient is improving but still has increased work of breathing. If CPAP is within AEMT scope in your system and the patient is alert cooperative and able to protect his airway CPAP at 5-10 cmH2O can provide significant benefit. Contraindications include altered mental status inability to protect airway vomiting facial trauma and hypotension.

question: A 22-year-old female college student with a history of asthma is in severe respiratory distress. Her roommate called 911 when her inhaler was not helping. She is sitting upright unable to speak more than one or two words between breaths. She is using accessory muscles and appears exhausted. Her lips have a slight bluish tint. Vital signs are BP 132/78 HR 138 RR 36 SpO2 84% on room air. On auscultation you hear very faint wheezing in only the upper lung fields and almost no air movement in the bases. This diminished wheezing is more concerning than loud wheezing. Why?
option_a: Diminished wheezing means the asthma is improving
option_b: Diminished or absent breath sounds in asthma indicates severe bronchospasm with minimal air movement. This is a silent chest and indicates impending respiratory failure. Loud wheezing actually requires air movement
option_c: The wheezing moved to her upper lungs which is better
option_d: She does not have asthma if wheezing is diminished
rationale: In severe asthma the absence of wheezing is an ominous sign. Wheezing is produced by turbulent airflow through narrowed airways but it requires airflow to be heard. When bronchospasm becomes so severe that almost no air can move the chest becomes silent. This silent chest indicates critical bronchospasm and impending respiratory failure. Other signs of impending respiratory failure include inability to speak altered mental status severe accessory muscle use and exhaustion. This patient needs immediate aggressive bronchodilator therapy high-flow oxygen and preparation for possible ventilatory assistance. Transport immediately.

question: You are aggressively treating the severe asthma patient with continuous albuterol nebulization and high-flow oxygen. Despite treatment her level of consciousness is decreasing and her respiratory rate has dropped from 36 to 16 with shallow breaths. SpO2 is 78%. She is no longer able to sit up and appears obtunded. What does this change in respiratory rate indicate and what must you do?
option_a: The slower respiratory rate means her asthma is improving
option_b: The decreasing respiratory rate and level of consciousness indicate respiratory failure. She is tiring out and can no longer maintain adequate ventilation. Begin assisted ventilations with BVM immediately
option_c: Continue albuterol and she will improve
option_d: Coach her to take deeper breaths
rationale: A decreasing respiratory rate in a patient with severe respiratory distress is an ominous sign indicating exhaustion and impending respiratory arrest. A patient who was breathing fast is now breathing slowly not because they are better but because their respiratory muscles are failing. The altered mental status confirms hypoxia and possibly hypercapnia. Begin immediate bag-valve-mask ventilation with high-flow oxygen. Ventilate at 10-12 breaths per minute with adequate volume to see chest rise. Be prepared for this patient to require advanced airway placement. Continue nebulized albuterol through the BVM circuit if possible.

question: A 58-year-old male with a history of heart failure presents with sudden onset severe shortness of breath that woke him from sleep. He is sitting bolt upright and cannot lie down without becoming more dyspneic. He is coughing up pink frothy sputum. He has bilateral crackles throughout both lung fields. Vital signs are BP 186/108 HR 118 RR 32 SpO2 84% on room air. His neck veins are distended and he has 2+ pitting edema of his lower extremities. What is causing his respiratory distress and what treatment is appropriate?
option_a: He has pneumonia and needs antibiotics
option_b: He has acute pulmonary edema from left heart failure. Treatment includes sitting him upright high-flow oxygen or CPAP if available and nitroglycerin if his blood pressure supports it
option_c: He is having an asthma attack and needs albuterol
option_d: He has a pulmonary embolism
rationale: Acute pulmonary edema occurs when the failing left ventricle cannot adequately pump blood forward causing backup into the pulmonary circulation. Fluid transudates into the alveoli impairing gas exchange. Classic presentation includes sudden severe dyspnea worse lying down called orthopnea pink frothy sputum bilateral crackles elevated JVP and peripheral edema. Treatment focuses on reducing preload and supporting oxygenation. Position upright to reduce venous return. High-flow oxygen or CPAP improves oxygenation and reduces preload. Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL reduces preload through venodilation but requires adequate blood pressure typically SBP greater than 100.

question: You are treating the pulmonary edema patient with CPAP at 10 cmH2O and have administered nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual. His SpO2 has improved to 92% and he states he can breathe a little easier. However his blood pressure has dropped from 186/108 to 94/62 and he is becoming more confused. What should you do?
option_a: Continue current treatment as this is expected improvement
option_b: Remove CPAP and discontinue nitroglycerin. The hypotension indicates he is becoming cardiogenic shock. Position supine or legs elevated and give a small fluid bolus if severely hypotensive
option_c: Give another nitroglycerin to continue lowering his blood pressure
option_d: Increase CPAP to 15 cmH2O
rationale: This patient has transitioned from hypertensive pulmonary edema to cardiogenic shock. While pulmonary edema with hypertension benefits from preload reduction with nitrates and CPAP the same treatments can be harmful when the patient becomes hypotensive. The sudden drop in blood pressure and altered mental status indicate inadequate cardiac output. Discontinue nitroglycerin as further preload reduction will worsen hypotension. CPAP may need to be reduced or removed as positive pressure decreases venous return. Position to improve perfusion. This patient needs rapid transport as he may require inotropic support or other advanced interventions not available at AEMT level.

question: A 72-year-old female who had hip replacement surgery 5 days ago presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. She has been mostly bedbound since surgery. She denies cough fever or leg pain but you notice her right calf appears slightly swollen compared to the left. She is anxious and diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 108/72 HR 118 RR 26 SpO2 89% on room air. Lung sounds are clear. What do you suspect and why are clear lung sounds significant?
option_a: She has pneumonia causing her symptoms
option_b: You should suspect pulmonary embolism. The recent surgery immobility sudden dyspnea pleuritic pain tachycardia hypoxia with clear lungs and unilateral leg swelling all suggest PE. Clear lungs with significant hypoxia indicates a non-pulmonary cause of hypoxemia
option_c: She is having a panic attack from being in the hospital
option_d: Her symptoms are normal after hip surgery
rationale: Pulmonary embolism PE should be suspected in any patient with risk factors for DVT such as recent surgery immobility cancer or prior clots who presents with sudden dyspnea and chest pain. The combination of significant hypoxia with clear lung sounds is an important clue because most causes of hypoxia pneumonia pulmonary edema COPD have abnormal lung sounds. In PE the lungs themselves are often clear because the problem is blocked pulmonary blood flow not alveolar disease. The swollen calf suggests DVT as the source. Treatment is supportive with oxygen and monitoring. Large PE can cause cardiovascular collapse.

question: A 56-year-old male is complaining of chest pressure that started 30 minutes ago while watching television. He describes it as a heaviness in the center of his chest radiating to his left arm and jaw rated 7 out of 10. He is diaphoretic and nauseated. He has a history of hypertension and high cholesterol and smokes one pack per day. His medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 158/94 HR 88 RR 18 SpO2 96% on room air. What is your primary concern and what initial treatment should you provide at the AEMT level?
option_a: This is indigestion from a large meal
option_b: This presentation is classic for acute coronary syndrome likely MI. Give aspirin 324 mg chewed establish IV access provide oxygen if hypoxic and give nitroglycerin if blood pressure permits and no contraindications
option_c: He is having a panic attack
option_d: His blood pressure is too high to be having a heart attack
rationale: This patient has classic acute coronary syndrome presentation with substernal pressure radiating to arm and jaw diaphoresis nausea and multiple cardiac risk factors. Immediate treatment includes aspirin 324 mg chewed to inhibit platelets supplemental oxygen if SpO2 is below 94% IV access and nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual every 5 minutes up to 3 doses if SBP remains above 100 and there are no contraindications. Contraindications to nitroglycerin include hypotension recent use of erectile dysfunction medications and suspected right ventricular infarction. Obtain 12-lead ECG if available and capable. Rapid transport to a cardiac-capable facility is essential.

question: You are treating the chest pain patient with suspected ACS. You have given aspirin 324 mg and obtained a 12-lead ECG which shows ST elevation in leads II III and aVF. Before giving nitroglycerin what additional assessment should you perform and why?
option_a: No additional assessment is needed before nitroglycerin
option_b: Inferior MI seen in leads II III aVF may involve the right ventricle. Obtain right-sided leads especially V4R if possible. Right ventricular infarction is preload-dependent and nitroglycerin can cause severe hypotension
option_c: Check his blood glucose before nitroglycerin
option_d: Assess his lung sounds for wheezing
rationale: Inferior wall MI is often associated with right ventricular involvement because both are typically supplied by the right coronary artery. The right ventricle depends on adequate preload to maintain cardiac output. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation which reduces preload. In a patient with RV infarction this preload reduction can cause profound hypotension and cardiovascular collapse. If possible obtain right-sided leads with ST elevation in V4R greater than 1mm being diagnostic of RV involvement. If right-sided leads are not available or show RV involvement use nitroglycerin cautiously if at all and be prepared to treat hypotension with IV fluids.

question: A 78-year-old female presents with weakness and fatigue that has been worsening over the past day. She denies chest pain but admits to some mild shortness of breath and nausea. She has a history of diabetes and hypertension. She takes metformin lisinopril and aspirin. Her skin is pale and slightly diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 104/68 HR 96 RR 20 SpO2 94% on room air. Blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. Given her age and medical history what should you consider and why might her presentation be atypical?
option_a: This is a viral illness causing her fatigue
option_b: Elderly patients and diabetics often have atypical ACS presentations without classic chest pain. Her weakness fatigue dyspnea and nausea with diaphoresis could represent acute MI. Obtain 12-lead ECG and treat as possible ACS
option_c: Her blood sugar is normal so this is not diabetes-related
option_d: She is just dehydrated and needs IV fluids
rationale: Elderly patients women and diabetics frequently present with atypical symptoms of acute coronary syndrome. Instead of classic crushing chest pain they may experience weakness fatigue dyspnea nausea or vague discomfort. Diabetic neuropathy can blunt cardiac pain perception. These atypical presentations result in delayed recognition and higher mortality. This patient has multiple risk factors age diabetes hypertension and symptoms that could represent ACS. Obtain a 12-lead ECG maintain high suspicion give aspirin if not already taking it and transport for evaluation. Do not dismiss vague symptoms in high-risk patients.

question: A 68-year-old male collapsed at a grocery store. Bystanders began CPR immediately. You arrive to find CPR in progress with an AED attached that has advised one shock was delivered. You take over CPR and attach your monitor which shows coarse ventricular fibrillation. What are your immediate actions and what is the AEMT role in cardiac arrest management?
option_a: AEMTs cannot manage cardiac arrest and should wait for paramedics
option_b: Continue high-quality CPR defibrillate at maximum energy establish IV or IO access administer epinephrine per protocols and continue CPR and defibrillation cycles. Prepare for advanced airway if needed and within scope
option_c: Only continue CPR until paramedics arrive
option_d: Stop CPR to assess for a pulse every minute
rationale: AEMTs play a crucial role in cardiac arrest management. Immediate priorities include high-quality CPR with minimal interruptions defibrillation for VF or pulseless VT every 2 minutes and establishing IV or IO access for medication administration. Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO is given every 3-5 minutes in cardiac arrest per protocols. Basic airway management with BVM is initially appropriate with advancement to supraglottic airway if unable to adequately ventilate. The chain of survival depends on early CPR early defibrillation and efficient resuscitation which AEMTs are trained to provide. If ACLS-level providers are available they can provide additional interventions.

question: You are managing the cardiac arrest patient. After the second shock and 2 minutes of CPR with epinephrine given the rhythm changes to a narrow complex rhythm at 78 bpm. You stop CPR to assess and find a weak pulse with BP 68/42. The patient is not breathing and remains unconscious. What has occurred and what are your priorities now?
option_a: Resume CPR because the blood pressure is too low
option_b: The patient has achieved ROSC return of spontaneous circulation. Priorities are maintaining airway and ventilation optimizing oxygenation supporting blood pressure with fluids and continued monitoring. Do not resume CPR if pulse is present
option_c: The rhythm is PEA and you should continue CPR
option_d: Terminate resuscitation since he is not breathing
rationale: Return of spontaneous circulation ROSC is indicated by a perfusing rhythm with palpable pulse. This patient has a pulse so CPR should not be performed. Post-ROSC priorities include airway management with continued BVM or advanced airway as needed ventilation targeting SpO2 94-98% blood pressure support targeting SBP greater than 90 with IV fluid bolus and continuous monitoring as rearrest is common. The hypotension requires treatment with crystalloid bolus. Transport rapidly to a facility capable of post-arrest care including targeted temperature management and cardiac catheterization if STEMI is present. Document time of ROSC and all interventions.

question: A 72-year-old female presents with palpitations and dizziness. She states her heart has been racing for about 2 hours and she feels like it might beat out of her chest. She has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. Vital signs are BP 138/88 HR 188 regular RR 18 SpO2 97% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia without visible P waves. She is anxious but alert and oriented. What is the most likely rhythm and what is the AEMT role in managing this patient?
option_a: This is sinus tachycardia from anxiety
option_b: This is likely supraventricular tachycardia SVT. At AEMT level provide supportive care oxygen IV access and consider vagal maneuvers if trained and protocolized. Monitor closely for deterioration and transport for definitive treatment
option_c: Give nitroglycerin for the fast heart rate
option_d: Cardiovert immediately
rationale: Regular narrow complex tachycardia at this rate without P waves is most likely SVT specifically AVNRT or AVRT. The patient is hemodynamically stable with adequate blood pressure and mental status. At AEMT level management includes supplemental oxygen if needed IV access for potential medication administration and monitoring. Some AEMT protocols include vagal maneuvers such as the Valsalva maneuver or modified Valsalva which can terminate SVT by increasing vagal tone. Adenosine and cardioversion are typically paramedic-level interventions but the AEMT provides essential supportive care and monitoring during transport.

question: A 68-year-old male presents with weakness and near-syncope. He states he almost passed out while walking to the bathroom. He has a history of heart disease and had a heart attack 2 years ago. He takes aspirin metoprolol lisinopril and atorvastatin. He appears pale and mildly diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 88/54 HR 38 regular RR 16 SpO2 95% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular bradycardia with wide QRS complexes. What is this patient experiencing and what is the AEMT level of care?
option_a: This is normal for someone taking metoprolol
option_b: He has symptomatic bradycardia with hemodynamic compromise. At AEMT level establish IV access give fluid bolus for hypotension position supine with legs elevated and transport rapidly. Atropine and pacing are ALS interventions but your supportive care is essential
option_c: His heart rate is not dangerously low
option_d: Give nitroglycerin for his low blood pressure
rationale: Symptomatic bradycardia means a slow heart rate causing symptoms such as hypotension syncope altered mental status or signs of shock. This patient's HR of 38 with BP 88/54 and near-syncope is symptomatic. The wide QRS suggests the escape rhythm is from below the AV node which often does not respond well to atropine. At AEMT level provide supportive care including IV access IV fluid bolus to support blood pressure positioning to optimize perfusion and continuous monitoring. Transcutaneous pacing and atropine are ALS interventions. Your role is stabilization and rapid transport to definitive care.

question: A 25-year-old male was eating at a Thai restaurant when he developed lip tingling followed by lip and tongue swelling. He has hives spreading across his trunk. He is mildly short of breath with some throat tightness. He states he has a tree nut allergy and thinks there may have been nuts in his food. Vital signs are BP 118/74 HR 104 RR 20 SpO2 96% on room air. He has diffuse urticaria and visible lip angioedema but can speak in full sentences without stridor. What severity is this allergic reaction and what is appropriate treatment?
option_a: This is a mild reaction that only needs diphenhydramine
option_b: This is anaphylaxis with involvement of multiple systems skin respiratory and should be treated with epinephrine 0.3 mg IM even though he is not yet in shock. Early epinephrine prevents progression
option_c: Since his blood pressure is normal this is not anaphylaxis
option_d: Have him use his EpiPen and he can refuse transport
rationale: Anaphylaxis is defined as a serious allergic reaction involving multiple organ systems that is rapid in onset and potentially fatal. This patient has skin involvement urticaria angioedema and respiratory involvement throat tightness dyspnea meeting criteria for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is indicated for anaphylaxis even before hemodynamic instability develops because early treatment prevents progression to severe anaphylaxis. The dose is 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration given IM in the lateral thigh. Do not wait for the patient to deteriorate before giving epinephrine. Adjunct treatments include diphenhydramine and monitoring but these do not replace epinephrine.

question: You have administered epinephrine 0.3 mg IM to the patient with anaphylaxis. Five minutes later his hives are slightly improved but he now has audible stridor and is having difficulty speaking. His SpO2 has dropped to 91% and he appears more anxious. Blood pressure is now 102/68 HR 124. What should you do?
option_a: The epinephrine did not work so try diphenhydramine instead
option_b: His symptoms are progressing despite initial epinephrine. Repeat epinephrine 0.3 mg IM provide high-flow oxygen and prepare for possible airway intervention. Establish IV access and give fluid bolus
option_c: Wait to see if the first epinephrine dose works
option_d: Give oral antihistamines
rationale: Epinephrine can and should be repeated in anaphylaxis if symptoms persist or worsen. The typical interval is every 5-15 minutes as needed. This patient has developing upper airway obstruction indicated by stridor and difficulty speaking which is life-threatening. Give a second dose of epinephrine IM immediately provide high-flow oxygen establish IV access and give a fluid bolus for the dropping blood pressure. The stridor indicates laryngeal edema and he may rapidly progress to complete airway obstruction. Be prepared for emergency airway intervention. Continue epinephrine and supportive care during rapid transport.

question: A 45-year-old female was stung by a bee and now has severe swelling of her entire arm and local hives around the sting site. She denies any shortness of breath throat tightness or difficulty swallowing. She has no hives elsewhere and feels fine other than pain and itching at the sting site. Vital signs are completely normal. She does not have a history of anaphylaxis. Does this patient need epinephrine?
option_a: Yes all bee stings require epinephrine because they can progress to anaphylaxis
option_b: No this is a large local reaction not anaphylaxis. Treatment includes diphenhydramine for itching ice for swelling and monitoring for any systemic symptoms that would indicate progression to anaphylaxis
option_c: Yes give epinephrine to prevent progression
option_d: No treatment is needed for any bee sting
rationale: Large local reactions are exaggerated local responses to insect stings with extensive swelling beyond the immediate sting site. While impressive appearing they are not anaphylaxis as long as symptoms remain localized and there are no systemic symptoms. Epinephrine is indicated for systemic reactions involving multiple organ systems not for local reactions even large ones. Treatment includes removing the stinger if present diphenhydramine 25-50 mg for itching ice or cold compresses and monitoring for any systemic symptoms. Advise the patient to seek immediate care if she develops any breathing difficulty throat symptoms widespread hives or dizziness.

question: A 72-year-old male was eating breakfast when his wife noticed he dropped his fork and his face looked crooked. His speech is slurred and he cannot raise his right arm. His wife called 911 immediately and states symptoms began approximately 20 minutes ago. He has a history of hypertension diabetes and atrial fibrillation. His medications include metoprolol metformin and warfarin. Vital signs are BP 178/98 HR 88 irregular RR 16 SpO2 97% on room air. Using the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale what findings suggest stroke and what is critical to document?
option_a: His symptoms are from low blood sugar and you should check glucose first
option_b: He has facial droop arm drift and speech abnormality all three components of CPSS are abnormal strongly suggesting stroke. Critical documentation includes exact time of symptom onset or last known well blood glucose and current medications especially anticoagulants
option_c: Wait to see if symptoms resolve before concluding this is a stroke
option_d: His blood pressure is too high for stroke
rationale: The Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale assesses three components: facial droop ask patient to smile arm drift hold arms out with eyes closed and speech repeat a sentence. Any one abnormality has 72% probability of stroke and all three abnormal has greater than 85% probability. This patient has all three. Critical documentation includes exact time of symptom onset needed for thrombolytic eligibility blood glucose to rule out hypoglycemia mimicking stroke current medications especially anticoagulants which affect treatment options and vital signs. Time is brain in stroke as neurons die rapidly. Alert the receiving hospital as a stroke alert.

question: You are transporting the stroke patient to a comprehensive stroke center. His symptoms have remained stable with right-sided weakness facial droop and dysarthria. His blood glucose is 138 mg/dL. His blood pressure is 182/96. Should you attempt to lower his blood pressure during transport?
option_a: Yes the elevated blood pressure is dangerous and should be lowered to normal
option_b: No allow permissive hypertension in stroke. Elevated BP may be maintaining perfusion to the ischemic brain tissue. Only treat if severely elevated typically greater than 220/120 for non-tPA candidates
option_c: Give aspirin to lower his blood pressure
option_d: The blood pressure does not matter in stroke
rationale: In acute ischemic stroke elevated blood pressure may be a compensatory mechanism to maintain blood flow to the ischemic penumbra the area of brain tissue at risk but not yet dead. Lowering blood pressure aggressively can reduce perfusion to this tissue and extend the stroke. Current guidelines allow permissive hypertension in stroke patients not receiving tPA up to 220/120. If the patient is a tPA candidate blood pressure must be below 185/110. At AEMT level do not administer antihypertensives for stroke unless specifically directed by medical control for severe hypertension. Focus on supportive care and rapid transport.

question: You respond to a 65-year-old female who had sudden onset of left arm weakness and slurred speech 30 minutes ago. On your arrival her symptoms have completely resolved and she feels fine. She wants to refuse transport stating it was probably nothing since she is better now. Vital signs are normal and neurological exam shows no deficits. Her husband is very concerned and wants her to go to the hospital. What was this event and what should you advise?
option_a: Since her symptoms resolved completely this was nothing to worry about
option_b: This was likely a transient ischemic attack TIA which is a warning sign for stroke. Up to 10-15% of TIA patients will have a stroke within 90 days with highest risk in the first 48 hours. Strongly recommend transport for urgent evaluation
option_c: She had a panic attack that resolved
option_d: If symptoms are gone no evaluation is needed
rationale: A transient ischemic attack TIA is a brief episode of neurological dysfunction from focal brain ischemia without infarction. Symptoms typically resolve within minutes to an hour. TIA is a medical emergency despite symptom resolution because it indicates unstable cerebrovascular disease with high short-term stroke risk. Up to 10-15% of TIA patients will have a stroke within 90 days with the highest risk in the first 24-48 hours. She needs urgent evaluation including brain imaging carotid studies cardiac evaluation and initiation of secondary prevention medications. Strongly encourage transport and document your advice if she refuses.

question: You respond to a 32-year-old male having a seizure at a bus stop. Bystanders state he suddenly fell down and started shaking about 3 minutes ago. On arrival he is having generalized tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerking of all extremities. He is unconscious with clenched jaw and drooling. He is cyanotic around the lips. There is urine on his pants. His wallet contains an epilepsy medical alert card. What are your immediate management priorities?
option_a: Insert an oral airway and restrain him to prevent injury
option_b: Protect him from injury do not restrain or put anything in his mouth position to protect airway suction if needed give oxygen and check blood glucose. Time the seizure and be prepared to give benzodiazepine if it continues
option_c: Give him a dose of his seizure medication
option_d: Wait for the seizure to stop then assess him
rationale: Immediate seizure management priorities are protecting the patient from injury by moving objects and cushioning the head do not restrain forcefully as this can cause injury. Do not put anything in the mouth as this can cause dental injury or obstruction. Position to protect the airway allowing secretions to drain suction if needed and provide supplemental oxygen. Check blood glucose as hypoglycemia can cause seizures. Time the seizure duration as seizures lasting greater than 5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery constitute status epilepticus requiring benzodiazepine treatment. Most seizures stop spontaneously within 1-2 minutes.

question: The seizure patient continues to have tonic-clonic activity. It has now been 6 minutes since seizure onset. His glucose is 118 mg/dL. He remains cyanotic with SpO2 84% and his seizure activity continues unabated. Your protocols authorize benzodiazepine administration for status epilepticus. What medication and dose is appropriate?
option_a: Diazepam 20 mg IV push
option_b: Midazolam 10 mg IM or 5 mg IV or per protocol. Midazolam IM is as effective as IV lorazepam and is preferred when IV access is difficult
option_c: Phenytoin 1000 mg IV is first-line for seizures
option_d: No medication should be given until the seizure stops
rationale: Status epilepticus is traditionally defined as seizure activity lasting greater than 5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment. Midazolam 10 mg IM is the preferred prehospital treatment when IV access is not established as IM absorption is rapid and reliable. If IV access is available midazolam 5 mg IV lorazepam 4 mg IV or diazepam 5-10 mg IV are options depending on protocols. Midazolam can also be given intranasally at 10 mg using a mucosal atomizer. Be prepared for respiratory depression after benzodiazepine administration and have BVM ready.

question: After midazolam 10 mg IM the seizure stops. The patient is now postictal making incomprehensible sounds and not following commands. His respirations are 10 per minute and shallow with SpO2 88% on high-flow oxygen. He is limp but withdraws from painful stimuli. What is the postictal period and how should you manage this patient?
option_a: The postictal period is when patients are most likely to have another seizure so give more midazolam
option_b: The postictal period is the recovery phase after a seizure characterized by confusion lethargy and sometimes focal deficits. His slow respirations are concerning for benzodiazepine effect combined with postictal state. Assist ventilations with BVM if needed monitor closely and be prepared for airway intervention
option_c: He should be fully alert immediately after the seizure stops
option_d: The postictal period is permanent brain damage from the seizure
rationale: The postictal period is a transient state following seizure characterized by confusion lethargy sometimes aggression focal neurological deficits called Todd paralysis and gradual return to baseline over minutes to hours. This patient has combined postictal depression and benzodiazepine effect causing respiratory depression. The respiratory rate of 10 with SpO2 88% requires intervention. Assist ventilations with BVM targeting SpO2 greater than 94% and adequate chest rise. Consider airway adjuncts if needed. Continue monitoring as he may improve as both the postictal state and benzodiazepine effects wear off. Transport for evaluation.

question: A 4-year-old girl had a generalized seizure lasting about 2 minutes that has now stopped. Her mother reports she has had fever for 2 days from an ear infection. The child is now crying and recognizes her mother. Temperature is 102.8F and other vital signs are appropriate for age. She has no history of seizures or epilepsy. What type of seizure was this and what should you tell the mother?
option_a: This child has epilepsy and will need lifelong medication
option_b: This was most likely a simple febrile seizure which occurs in children 6 months to 5 years associated with fever. Simple febrile seizures are benign with excellent prognosis and most children do not develop epilepsy. Transport for evaluation of the fever source
option_c: Febrile seizures cause brain damage
option_d: This is not a seizure because it was caused by fever
rationale: Simple febrile seizures are the most common seizure type in children occurring in 2-5% of children between 6 months and 5 years. They are associated with fever usually from viral illness are generalized last less than 15 minutes and do not recur within 24 hours. Prognosis is excellent with no increased risk of developmental problems and only slightly higher risk of epilepsy 2-4% vs 1% in general population. The child should be transported for evaluation of the fever source in this case ear infection. Reassure the mother that febrile seizures while frightening to witness are generally benign.

question: A 78-year-old female is found by her daughter confused and difficult to arouse. The daughter states her mother was fine when she spoke to her on the phone last night. The patient lives alone and has a history of diabetes hypertension and urinary incontinence issues. She is lethargic and oriented only to name. She is warm to touch and her breathing is slightly rapid. Vital signs are BP 98/62 HR 108 RR 22 SpO2 95% Temp 101.8F. Blood glucose is 168 mg/dL. Urine has a foul odor. What is the most likely cause of her altered mental status?
option_a: She is having a stroke
option_b: She most likely has a urinary tract infection progressing to sepsis. UTI is the most common cause of altered mental status in elderly patients. The fever tachycardia hypotension and foul-smelling urine support this
option_c: She has dementia and this is her baseline
option_d: Her blood sugar is too high and she has DKA
rationale: Urinary tract infection is the most common cause of acute altered mental status in elderly patients especially women. Elderly patients often present with atypical symptoms of infection without classic symptoms like dysuria or frequency. Instead they present with confusion lethargy weakness or falls. This patient has signs of sepsis from UTI including fever tachycardia hypotension and altered mental status. The foul-smelling urine supports UTI as the source. Treatment includes IV access fluid bolus for hypotension and sepsis and rapid transport. Consider early notification of possible sepsis to the receiving facility.

question: A 45-year-old male is found unresponsive by coworkers at a construction site. He was working in the heat and collapsed. He is unresponsive to voice but withdraws from pain. His skin is hot dry and flushed. Vital signs are BP 102/68 HR 132 RR 28 SpO2 94% Temp by temporal reading is 104.8F. His coworkers say he complained of headache and nausea before becoming confused and then collapsing. What is happening and what is the treatment priority?
option_a: He is having heat exhaustion and needs to rest in shade
option_b: He has heat stroke which is a life-threatening emergency. The hot dry skin altered mental status and temperature greater than 104F indicate thermoregulatory failure. Immediate aggressive cooling is the priority
option_c: This is heat cramps from electrolyte loss
option_d: He is having a diabetic emergency
rationale: Heat stroke is defined by core temperature greater than 104F with central nervous system dysfunction. Classic heat stroke often presents with hot dry skin indicating loss of the sweating mechanism though exertional heat stroke may still have sweating. This is a life-threatening emergency with mortality exceeding 50% if cooling is delayed. Immediate treatment priorities are aggressive cooling including removing clothing applying ice packs to neck axillae and groin wetting skin and fanning to promote evaporative cooling and cold IV fluids if available. Target temperature below 102F within 30 minutes. Altered mental status should improve with cooling.

question: A 52-year-old male was found by police in an alley. He is confused and staggering with slurred speech. He has an odor of alcohol on his breath. His clothes are disheveled and he has no identification. He is verbally abusive and refuses to cooperate with your exam. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 96 RR 16 SpO2 96%. Blood glucose is 78 mg/dL. You notice a healing laceration on his forehead. Why is it dangerous to assume this patient is simply intoxicated?
option_a: Alcohol intoxication is not dangerous and does not require treatment
option_b: Multiple conditions can mimic intoxication including hypoglycemia head injury stroke postictal state diabetic emergencies and toxic ingestions. The head laceration raises concern for traumatic brain injury. Assume the worst diagnose and transport for evaluation
option_c: If he smells like alcohol he is definitely just drunk
option_d: He is being difficult so he does not need medical care
rationale: Altered mental status with alcohol odor should never be simply attributed to intoxication until other causes are excluded. Life-threatening conditions that can mimic intoxication include hypoglycemia head injury especially subdural hematoma in intoxicated patients who fall stroke postictal state diabetic emergencies overdose and sepsis. This patient has a head laceration suggesting recent trauma and possible intracranial injury. Intoxicated patients are at high risk for falls and resultant head injury. His blood glucose is normal but he still needs evaluation. Do not let alcohol odor prevent thorough assessment and appropriate transport.

question: A 24-year-old male is found unresponsive in a park bathroom by security. There is drug paraphernalia nearby including a syringe and burnt spoon. He is unresponsive to all stimuli. His respirations are 6 per minute and shallow. His pupils are pinpoint and equal. His skin is pale and cyanotic around the lips. Vital signs are BP 88/54 HR 52 RR 6 SpO2 76%. What is the likely cause and what is the AEMT treatment?
option_a: He is sleeping and should be left alone
option_b: This is opioid overdose causing respiratory depression. Immediately ventilate with BVM and high-flow oxygen then administer naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV IM or intranasal. The priority is ventilation as hypoxia kills faster than you can give naloxone
option_c: Give naloxone 10 mg IV immediately
option_d: Check his blood sugar and give dextrose
rationale: Opioid overdose causes the classic triad of respiratory depression decreased level of consciousness and pinpoint pupils. This patient is critically hypoxic and hypoventilating. The immediate priority is restoring oxygenation through BVM ventilation with high-flow oxygen. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist that reverses opioid effects. Give naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV IM or intranasal while continuing to assist ventilations. Naloxone onset is 2-5 minutes depending on route. The goal is to restore adequate spontaneous respirations not necessarily full consciousness. Titrate to respiratory effort to avoid precipitating severe withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients.

question: You have given naloxone 2 mg IV to the opioid overdose patient. Within 3 minutes he awakens suddenly and becomes extremely agitated pulling at his IV and trying to get up. He is sweating complaining of severe abdominal cramps and muscle aches. He is angry and wants to leave immediately. His respirations are now 18 per minute and SpO2 is 96%. What is happening and what should you counsel him about?
option_a: The naloxone caused these symptoms and you gave too much
option_b: He is experiencing acute opioid withdrawal precipitated by naloxone. More importantly naloxone has a shorter duration 30-90 minutes than most opioids so he is at risk for re-sedation and recurrent respiratory depression when the naloxone wears off. He needs monitoring
option_c: He is fine now and can safely leave
option_d: Give more naloxone to prevent recurrence
rationale: Naloxone reverses opioid effects by competitively binding opioid receptors displacing the drug. In opioid-dependent patients this precipitates acute withdrawal with agitation nausea vomiting diarrhea abdominal cramps diaphoresis and piloerection. This is uncomfortable but not life-threatening. More importantly naloxone has a shorter duration 30-90 minutes than many opioids especially long-acting formulations or fentanyl analogs. When the naloxone wears off the remaining opioid can cause recurrent respiratory depression and death. This patient needs transport and monitoring. If he refuses document thoroughly and ensure he understands the re-sedation risk.

question: A 35-year-old female is found confused and agitated by her husband. He states she has been depressed and found empty pill bottles of her amitriptyline a tricyclic antidepressant nearby. She is disoriented and picking at things that are not there. Her skin is hot dry and flushed. Her pupils are dilated. She has dry mouth. Heart rate is 132 and the rhythm on the monitor shows a wide QRS complex. Vital signs are BP 88/62 HR 132 RR 20 SpO2 96% Temp 100.8F. What toxidrome is this and what rhythm abnormality is concerning?
option_a: This is opioid overdose and she needs naloxone
option_b: This is anticholinergic toxidrome hot as a hare dry as a bone red as a beet blind as a bat mad as a hatter. The wide QRS complex indicates cardiac sodium channel blockade from TCA which can progress to fatal arrhythmias. This is a critical poisoning
option_c: This is serotonin syndrome
option_d: She is having a psychiatric emergency
rationale: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes anticholinergic toxidrome with the classic findings remembered as hot as a hare hyperthermia dry as a bone dry skin and mucous membranes red as a beet flushed skin blind as a bat dilated pupils and mad as a hatter altered mental status hallucinations. The cardiac toxicity is life-threatening. TCAs block sodium channels causing QRS widening and can progress to ventricular arrhythmias. QRS greater than 100ms indicates significant toxicity. At AEMT level provide supportive care IV access monitoring and rapid transport. Sodium bicarbonate is the specific treatment but is typically ALS or hospital level.

question: A 28-year-old male is reported by family to be behaving erratically. He has been awake for 3 days appears paranoid and believes people are trying to kill him. He is agitated pacing and talking rapidly. His speech is pressured and he jumps from topic to topic. He has a history of bipolar disorder and stopped taking his lithium 2 weeks ago. He is becoming increasingly threatening toward family members. Vital signs are BP 152/94 HR 112 RR 18 SpO2 98% Temp 98.8F. What is likely happening and what is the safety priority?
option_a: He is having a panic attack and needs reassurance
option_b: He is likely experiencing an acute manic episode with psychotic features. Safety is the priority for you your partner the patient and family. Maintain safe distance do not corner him speak calmly and prepare for potential need for chemical or physical restraint
option_c: He is intoxicated on stimulants
option_d: Immediately restrain him before he hurts someone
rationale: This patient shows signs of acute mania including decreased need for sleep pressured speech racing thoughts and paranoid delusions which can occur in severe mania. The paranoia and threatening behavior make him potentially dangerous. Safety priorities include maintaining safe distance keeping exits accessible using calm non-confrontational communication avoiding arguing with delusions and having law enforcement available if needed. Request additional resources before attempting transport. Chemical sedation may be needed if he cannot be safely transported. At AEMT level your role is scene safety and de-escalation with transport for psychiatric evaluation and medical clearance.

question: You respond to a 22-year-old female who according to her roommate took a bunch of pills about 30 minutes ago. The patient appears drowsy but arousable. There are empty pill bottles nearby including acetaminophen and ibuprofen. She admits she wanted to hurt herself because her boyfriend broke up with her. She now states she regrets it and is scared. Vital signs are normal. She is cooperative and wants help. What are the medical and psychiatric priorities for this patient?
option_a: Since she regrets it and her vital signs are normal she can refuse transport
option_b: She needs transport for both medical and psychiatric evaluation. Acetaminophen overdose may not show symptoms initially but can cause fatal liver failure days later. She also needs psychiatric evaluation for suicidal ideation. Do not leave her alone
option_c: Her vital signs are normal so the overdose was not serious
option_d: Have her drink milk to neutralize the pills
rationale: This patient has two critical needs: medical evaluation for potential acetaminophen toxicity and psychiatric evaluation for suicidal ideation. Acetaminophen overdose is particularly dangerous because patients are often asymptomatic initially but can develop fatal hepatic failure 24-72 hours later. The antidote N-acetylcysteine is most effective when given within 8 hours. Even if her vital signs are normal she needs evaluation and possible treatment. The psychiatric component requires evaluation for suicidal ideation risk assessment and appropriate intervention. Do not leave a suicidal patient alone and transport to a facility capable of both medical and psychiatric care.

question: A 32-year-old male is found by police running naked in traffic. He is extremely agitated screaming incoherently and fighting with officers. Despite being tased twice and restrained by four officers he continues to struggle with apparent superhuman strength. He is sweating profusely and his skin is hot to touch. His eyes are wild and he is making grunting sounds. Police report finding drug paraphernalia at his apartment. Vital signs are difficult to obtain but heart rate appears very rapid. What syndrome should you suspect and what is the danger?
option_a: This is psychiatric emergency only and he needs to go to a psychiatric facility
option_b: This is likely excited delirium syndrome. This is a medical emergency with risk of sudden death particularly from restraint and hyperthermia. Cool him down watch for sudden cardiac arrest and avoid prone restraint which can cause positional asphyxia
option_c: He is just high on drugs and will calm down eventually
option_d: Apply more physical restraint to control him
rationale: Excited delirium is a syndrome of extreme agitation hyperthermia superhuman strength pain tolerance and bizarre behavior often associated with stimulant drugs or psychiatric illness. It carries high risk of sudden death especially during restraint. The mechanism includes catecholamine surge hyperthermia acidosis and positional asphyxia from prone restraint. Management priorities include recognizing the medical emergency active cooling chemical sedation if available and within scope and avoiding prolonged prone restraint or pressure on the chest and abdomen. Place in lateral position as soon as possible. Monitor closely for sudden cardiac arrest. This is a medical emergency not just a behavioral problem.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain that started around his umbilicus yesterday and has now localized to his right lower abdomen. He has had nausea and vomited twice. He has no appetite. He has low-grade fever of 100.4F. Vital signs are BP 138/88 HR 98 RR 18 SpO2 98%. On exam his abdomen is tender in the right lower quadrant with guarding and he has rebound tenderness. He points to a spot between his umbilicus and right hip crest as the most painful area. What condition is most likely and what is this point called?
option_a: He has gastroenteritis and needs antiemetics
option_b: This is classic appendicitis. The point of maximal tenderness is McBurney point located one-third the distance from the right anterior superior iliac spine to the umbilicus. The migration of pain from periumbilical to RLQ rebound tenderness and fever strongly suggest appendicitis
option_c: He has a kidney stone
option_d: He has food poisoning
rationale: Appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain that migrates to the right lower quadrant as inflammation progresses to involve the parietal peritoneum. McBurney point is the classic location of maximal tenderness. Associated symptoms include anorexia nausea vomiting and low-grade fever. Rebound tenderness indicates peritoneal inflammation. This patient needs surgical evaluation as untreated appendicitis can progress to perforation and peritonitis. At AEMT level treatment is supportive including IV access positioning for comfort possibly IV antiemetics if available and transport. NPO status nothing by mouth should be maintained in case surgery is needed.

question: A 62-year-old female with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with severe diffuse abdominal pain that she says is worse than any pain she has ever experienced. She rates it 10 out of 10. Surprisingly when you examine her abdomen it is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds. She is writhing in pain and appears very ill. Vital signs are BP 108/72 HR 118 irregular RR 22 SpO2 95%. She takes warfarin for her atrial fibrillation. What condition should you suspect and why is the physical exam misleading?
option_a: Her exam is normal so her pain is exaggerated
option_b: Suspect mesenteric ischemia or ischemic bowel. The classic presentation is pain out of proportion to physical exam findings. Her atrial fibrillation is a risk factor for arterial embolism to the mesenteric vessels. This is a vascular emergency with high mortality
option_c: She has irritable bowel syndrome
option_d: The pain is from her warfarin
rationale: Mesenteric ischemia occurs when blood flow to the intestines is compromised usually from arterial embolism arterial thrombosis or venous thrombosis. Atrial fibrillation is a major risk factor as clots can embolize from the heart to the superior mesenteric artery. The classic presentation is severe abdominal pain out of proportion to physical examination findings. Early in the course the bowel is ischemic but not yet necrotic so the abdomen may be soft and non-tender despite severe pain. Later peritonitis develops as bowel necrosis occurs. This is a time-critical vascular emergency with mortality exceeding 60-80%. Rapid transport for surgical evaluation is essential.

question: A 55-year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents with vomiting blood. He has vomited approximately 500 mL of bright red blood and blood clots since symptoms began 1 hour ago. He has a history of liver cirrhosis. He appears pale and is anxious. Vital signs are BP 92/58 HR 128 RR 22 SpO2 96%. His abdomen is distended with a fluid wave. He has visible dilated veins on his abdominal wall. What is the likely source of bleeding and what is the treatment priority?
option_a: He has a stomach ulcer and needs antacids
option_b: He likely has bleeding esophageal varices from portal hypertension due to cirrhosis. This is life-threatening hemorrhage. Priorities are airway protection given risk of massive hematemesis large-bore IV access fluid resuscitation and rapid transport
option_c: He has food poisoning
option_d: Give him water to dilute the blood
rationale: Esophageal varices are dilated veins in the esophagus that develop from portal hypertension usually due to cirrhosis. When these varices rupture they can cause massive life-threatening hemorrhage. Signs of portal hypertension include ascites fluid wave and caput medusae dilated abdominal wall veins. This patient is in hemorrhagic shock with hypotension and tachycardia. Priorities include airway protection as these patients can vomit massive amounts of blood large-bore IV access two lines if possible crystalloid fluid resuscitation per hemorrhagic shock protocols and rapid transport. Definitive treatment requires endoscopy for banding or sclerotherapy and possible balloon tamponade.

question: A 68-year-old male with end-stage renal disease on dialysis presents with weakness and palpitations. He missed his last two dialysis sessions. He feels generally weak and slightly short of breath. His lower extremities are edematous. Vital signs are BP 158/94 HR 52 irregular RR 20 SpO2 93% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a slow irregular rhythm with peaked T waves and widened QRS complexes. What electrolyte abnormality is most likely and why is this immediately life-threatening?
option_a: He has low potassium from not eating
option_b: He has hyperkalemia from missing dialysis. The ECG changes peaked T waves wide QRS indicate cardiac toxicity from elevated potassium which can progress to fatal arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation and asystole
option_c: His symptoms are from fluid overload only
option_d: The bradycardia is normal for someone his age
rationale: Dialysis patients who miss treatments develop hyperkalemia because the kidneys cannot excrete potassium. ECG changes progress as potassium rises: peaked T waves at 5.5-6.5 mEq/L prolonged PR and QRS widening at 6.5-7.5 mEq/L loss of P waves and sine wave pattern at levels above 8 mEq/L followed by VF or asystole. This is immediately life-threatening. At AEMT level establish IV access avoid any potassium-containing fluids like lactated Ringer's monitor cardiac rhythm closely and transport emergently. If cardiac arrest occurs standard ACLS plus calcium chloride or gluconate if available stabilizes the cardiac membrane. Alert the hospital to prepare for emergent dialysis.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with severe colicky flank pain radiating to his groin. The pain started suddenly 2 hours ago and comes in waves. He is pacing and cannot find a comfortable position. He is nauseated and has vomited once. He has a history of kidney stones. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 104 RR 18 SpO2 98%. His abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the left CVA region. Urine shows blood on dipstick. What is the likely diagnosis and what is appropriate AEMT treatment?
option_a: He has appendicitis
option_b: He most likely has a kidney stone or nephrolithiasis. Treatment includes IV access IV fluid bolus antiemetics if available and analgesics for pain if within protocol. Patients with kidney stones often cannot get comfortable and may pace or writhe unlike peritonitis where patients lie still
option_c: He has a urinary tract infection
option_d: He needs emergency surgery
rationale: Kidney stones cause severe colicky pain as the stone passes through the ureter. The pain typically radiates from flank to groin following the ureteral path. Patients are often unable to find a comfortable position and pace or writhe which differentiates renal colic from peritonitis where patients lie still to avoid aggravating peritoneal inflammation. Hematuria blood in urine from ureteral mucosal trauma is common. At AEMT level treatment includes IV access IV fluid bolus which may help flush the stone and dilute urine antiemetics for nausea like ondansetron if available and pain medication per protocols. Most stones pass spontaneously but transport for imaging and pain management is appropriate.

question: A 65-year-old female presents with altered mental status. Her husband states she has been increasingly confused and lethargic over the past few days. She has had constipation and complains of muscle aches. Her face appears puffy and her voice is hoarse and slow. Her skin is dry and cool. She moves and speaks very slowly. Vital signs are BP 98/64 HR 52 RR 12 SpO2 92% Temp 96.2F. She takes levothyroxine but her husband thinks she may have run out several weeks ago. What condition should you suspect?
option_a: She has hypothermia from being outside
option_b: She likely has severe hypothyroidism or myxedema coma. Signs include altered mental status hypothermia bradycardia hypotension puffy face coarse skin and slow movements and speech. This is life-threatening and requires supportive care and hospital treatment
option_c: She has dementia
option_d: Her symptoms are normal aging changes
rationale: Myxedema coma is severe decompensated hypothyroidism that is life-threatening. It often occurs when hypothyroid patients stop taking thyroid medication or experience physiological stress. Classic features include altered mental status hypothermia sometimes profound bradycardia hypotension hypoventilation facial puffiness myxedema dry coarse skin and slowed movements and speech. Precipitants include cold exposure infection medication non-compliance and illness. At AEMT level treatment is supportive including gentle passive rewarming avoid active rewarming which can cause cardiovascular collapse airway support oxygen IV access with glucose and fluid and transport. Definitive treatment requires IV thyroid hormone.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with fever productive cough and shortness of breath for 3 days. His cough produces rust-colored sputum. He has right-sided chest pain that worsens with breathing. He has a history of COPD and diabetes. Vital signs are BP 102/68 HR 108 RR 26 SpO2 89% on room air Temp 102.4F. Lung sounds reveal crackles and decreased breath sounds at the right base. What is the likely diagnosis and what makes this patient high-risk?
option_a: He has bronchitis and just needs cough medicine
option_b: He has community-acquired pneumonia. Rust-colored sputum suggests pneumococcal pneumonia. His diabetes and COPD put him at high risk for complications. He shows signs of sepsis with fever tachycardia and borderline hypotension
option_c: He is having a COPD exacerbation only
option_d: His symptoms will resolve without treatment
rationale: Pneumonia presents with fever productive cough dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Rust-colored sputum is classic for Streptococcus pneumoniae. This patient has multiple risk factors including COPD which impairs lung defenses and diabetes which impairs immune function. His vital signs show concerning features suggestive of sepsis including fever tachycardia borderline hypotension and tachypnea. Treatment includes supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 greater than 92% IV access and fluid bolus if hypotensive. Monitor for deterioration. He needs antibiotics promptly at the hospital. Consider early notification to the hospital if sepsis is suspected.

question: A 22-year-old college student presents with severe headache fever and stiff neck. His roommate states he has been sick for 2 days but became confused today. He has a rash on his trunk and extremities that appears as small red spots that do not blanch with pressure. He is photophobic and keeps his eyes closed. Vital signs are BP 96/58 HR 124 RR 24 SpO2 96% Temp 103.8F. He is oriented to name only and has obvious neck stiffness with pain on flexion. What do you suspect and what precautions are needed?
option_a: He has the flu and needs rest and fluids
option_b: This presentation suggests bacterial meningitis possibly meningococcal given the petechial rash. Use droplet precautions mask on patient and you and treat for sepsis with IV fluids. This is life-threatening and highly time-sensitive. Alert the hospital
option_c: He has a migraine from stress
option_d: The rash is from an allergy
rationale: Bacterial meningitis presents with fever headache neck stiffness and altered mental status. The petechial non-blanching rash is highly concerning for meningococcemia Neisseria meningitidis which can progress rapidly to septic shock DIC and death within hours. Meningitis is spread via respiratory droplets so place a mask on the patient and wear a mask yourself. This patient has signs of sepsis requiring IV access and fluid resuscitation. Alert the hospital so they can prepare for isolation and immediate antibiotic administration. You may need post-exposure prophylaxis so document the exposure and notify your supervisor.

question: A 72-year-old female presents after a fall. Her husband states she has been having falls frequently over the past few months. She has shuffling gait stooped posture and her hands have a resting tremor. Her face appears expressionless. She moves slowly and has difficulty getting out of her chair. She has no apparent injury from the fall. She has a history of Parkinson disease and takes carbidopa-levodopa. What features of Parkinson disease contribute to her fall risk and what should you assess?
option_a: Her falls are just from normal aging
option_b: Parkinson disease causes bradykinesia slow movement rigidity resting tremor and postural instability which all contribute to fall risk. Assess for injuries from this and prior falls check blood pressure lying and standing for orthostatic hypotension which is common in Parkinson and ensure she is taking her medications regularly
option_c: She is falling on purpose for attention
option_d: Her medications are causing the falls
rationale: Parkinson disease causes motor symptoms that significantly increase fall risk including bradykinesia which impairs reaction time to prevent falls rigidity which limits protective movements postural instability which impairs balance and festinating gait shuffling hurrying steps. Additionally Parkinson patients often have orthostatic hypotension from autonomic dysfunction and from medications. Check orthostatic blood pressure lying and standing. These patients are also at risk for complications from falls including hip fractures and subdural hematomas. Ensure she has not sustained occult injuries and that she is compliant with her medications.

question: A 35-year-old hiker is found by his companions at a mountain campsite above 10000 feet. He ascended rapidly yesterday and has developed severe headache nausea vomiting and is now confused and ataxic stumbling gait. His companions note he was fine yesterday at lower altitude. Vital signs are BP 142/88 HR 102 RR 24 SpO2 86% on room air. His lips are cyanotic and he has crackles in his lungs. What altitude illness does he have and what is the treatment?
option_a: He has altitude sickness and needs to rest at this altitude
option_b: He has high altitude cerebral edema HACE and possibly high altitude pulmonary edema HAPE. This is life-threatening. Immediate treatment is descent to lower altitude supplemental oxygen and dexamethasone if available. Do not wait for improvement at current altitude
option_c: He has the flu from another hiker
option_d: His symptoms will improve with acclimatization if he waits
rationale: High altitude cerebral edema HACE presents with severe headache ataxia and altered mental status and represents severe altitude illness. The presence of pulmonary crackles with hypoxia suggests concurrent high altitude pulmonary edema HAPE. Both conditions are life-threatening. The only definitive treatment is descent to lower altitude even 1000-2000 feet can be lifesaving. Supplemental oxygen improves symptoms. Dexamethasone 8mg then 4mg every 6 hours treats HACE. Nifedipine treats HAPE. At your level provide oxygen assist with descent and transport to lower altitude and medical care. Do not wait for improvement without descent.

question: You respond to a 50-year-old male with general weakness. On assessment you find his vital signs are normal his blood glucose is 92 mg/dL and physical exam reveals no obvious abnormality. He is vague about his symptoms stating he just does not feel right and has had weakness for a few days. He has no significant medical history. He is reluctant to go to the hospital and states he probably just needs rest. What is the appropriate approach to this patient?
option_a: Tell him his exam is normal and he does not need transport
option_b: Take his concerns seriously complete a thorough assessment and recommend transport for evaluation. Vague symptoms in a 50-year-old male can represent serious conditions including cardiac disease malignancy or other occult illness. Document your findings recommendations and his response
option_c: He is wasting EMS resources with vague complaints
option_d: His symptoms are probably psychiatric
rationale: Vague symptoms like weakness and not feeling right in middle-aged adults should be taken seriously. Many serious conditions including acute coronary syndrome malignancy infections and metabolic disorders can present with nonspecific symptoms early in their course. A thorough assessment including history vital signs physical exam and blood glucose is essential. Even if findings are normal the patient should be offered transport for further evaluation. If he refuses thoroughly document your assessment findings the advice you provided and his informed refusal. Never dismiss patient concerns based on normal field findings as prehospital assessment has significant limitations.
